name: Barrios de Luna Reservoir and La Robla Loop
name_original: Embalse barrios de luna desde Pola de Gordón – circular por La Magdalena y La Robla
description: This difficult 46.8-mile road cycling route circles Embalse de los Barrios de Luna, offering stunning mountain views and a challenging ride.
region_intro: Road cycling routes around La Pola de Gordón are situated within the Cantabrian Mountains in León, Spain, offering diverse terrain for cyclists. The region is characterized by its mountainous landscape, including demanding climbs and flat sections, with many routes on paved surfaces. It is part of the Alto Bernesga Biosphere Reserve, featuring river valleys like the Bernesga and dramatic limestone gorges such as the Hoces de Vegacervera. This varied topography provides a range of experiences for those seeking the best road cycling in La Pola de Gordón. Best road cycling routes around La Pola De Gordón The most popular road cycling route is Barrios de Luna Reservoir and La Robla Loop, a 46.7 miles (75.2 km) trail that takes 3 hours 48 minutes to complete. This difficult route features significant elevation gain and views of the reservoir. Another top favourite among local road cyclists is Alto Bernesga and Los Argüellos Biospheres Loop, a difficult 66.0 miles (106.2 km) path. This route traverses the protected biosphere reserves, offering expansive mountain scenery. Local road cyclists also love the Lorenzana from La Robla - circular via Tapia de la Ribera and Selga de Ordás Reservoir, a 35.9 miles (57.9 km) trail leading through river valleys and past the Selga de Ordás Reservoir, often completed in about 2 hours 58 minutes. Road cycling around La Pola De Gordón is defined by the Cantabrian Mountains, the Bernesga River Valley, and impressive limestone gorges. The network offers options for both moderate and difficult ability levels. The routes in La Pola De Gordón are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 5.0 stars from over 5 reviews. More than 1000 road cyclists have used komoot to explore La Pola De Gordón's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many road cycling routes are available around La Pola de Gordón?
A: There are over 25 dedicated road cycling routes around La Pola de Gordón, offering a variety of experiences. These routes are highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 5.0 stars from over 5 reviews, and have been explored by more than 1000 road cyclists.

Q: What kind of terrain can I expect on road cycling routes in La Pola de Gordón?
A: The region offers diverse terrain, from gentle, low-traffic paths to challenging mountain ascents within the Cantabrian Mountains. You'll find a mix of flat stretches for speed and demanding climbs that test endurance, with most routes primarily paved for a smooth experience. The area is part of the Alto Bernesga Biosphere Reserve, providing stunning natural backdrops.

Q: Are there any easy or moderate road cycling routes for beginners or those seeking a less strenuous ride?
A: Yes, while the region is known for its challenging climbs, there are 16 moderate routes available. For example, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1046681315/lorenzana-from-la-robla-circular-via-tapia-de-la-ribera-and-selga-de-ordas-reservoir" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Lorenzana from La Robla - circular via Tapia de la Ribera and Selga de Ordás Reservoir&lt;/a&gt; is a moderate 57.9 km (35.9 miles) route that takes about 2 hours 58 minutes, leading through river valleys and past the reservoir.

Q: What are some challenging road cycling routes for experienced riders?
A: For experienced cyclists, La Pola de Gordón offers 11 difficult routes with significant elevation gain. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1042304517/alto-bernesga-and-los-argueellos-biospheres-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Alto Bernesga and Los Argüellos Biospheres Loop&lt;/a&gt; is a challenging 106.2 km (66.0 miles) path traversing protected biosphere reserves with expansive mountain scenery. Another demanding option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1042316978/barrios-de-luna-reservoir-and-la-robla-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Barrios de Luna Reservoir and La Robla Loop&lt;/a&gt;, a 75.2 km (46.7 miles) route featuring significant elevation gain and reservoir views.

Q: What natural attractions can I see along the road cycling routes?
A: Many routes offer views of the dramatic "Hoces de Vegacervera" (Gorges of Vegacervera), a spectacular canyon carved by the Torío River with impressive limestone walls. You can also cycle through the Alto Bernesga Biosphere Reserve, known for its untamed nature. The journey to the Valporquero Caves, though steep, is scenic, with the Torío River winding through dense forests. For panoramic views, consider routes that ascend towards areas like &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/859306" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Cruz de San Salvador&lt;/a&gt; or &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5556607" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Pico Fontañán Summit&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: Are there any notable mountain passes or peaks accessible by road bike?
A: Yes, the region is part of the Cantabrian Mountains and features several mountain passes and peaks. Cyclists can experience passes like &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/6335590" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Aralla Pass&lt;/a&gt; or &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5516331" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Collada Gistreo&lt;/a&gt;. While some peaks might require a hike, the ascents to areas like Cueto San Mateo offer some of the best views across northern León.

Q: What is the best time of year for road cycling in La Pola de Gordón?
A: The region's weather is generally favorable for cycling from spring through autumn. Spring offers lush greenery, while autumn provides stunning colors, especially in areas like El Faedo de Ciñera. Summer is also popular, but be prepared for potentially warmer temperatures on exposed sections. Always check local weather forecasts before heading out.

Q: Are there any circular road cycling routes?
A: Yes, many routes in the area are circular, allowing you to start and end in the same location. Examples include the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1046681315/lorenzana-from-la-robla-circular-via-tapia-de-la-ribera-and-selga-de-ordas-reservoir" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Lorenzana from La Robla - circular via Tapia de la Ribera and Selga de Ordás Reservoir&lt;/a&gt; and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1042300095/villamanin-from-la-robla-circular-by-torio-river-and-la-profunda-mine" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Villamanín from La Robla - circular by Torío river and La Profunda Mine&lt;/a&gt;, which passes by the Torío River and La Profunda Mine.

Q: Where can I park my car when going road cycling in La Pola de Gordón?
A: La Pola de Gordón and surrounding towns often have public parking areas. For specific routes, it's advisable to check the starting point details on komoot for information on nearby parking facilities. Many routes begin in towns like La Robla or La Pola de Gordón itself, where parking is generally available.

Q: Can I visit the Valporquero Caves after a ride?
A: Yes, the Valporquero Caves are a unique natural attraction located at the end of a scenic, steep road. After a ride, you can visit this geologically young cave system featuring magnificent stalactites, stalagmites, and vast chambers. For more information on the caves, you can visit &lt;a href="https://www.atlasobscura.com/places/valporquero-cave-spain" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Atlas Obscura&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: What is the average distance and duration of road cycling routes in this area?
A: The road cycling routes around La Pola de Gordón vary significantly in length and duration. Moderate routes, such as the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1046681315/lorenzana-from-la-robla-circular-via-tapia-de-la-ribera-and-selga-de-ordas-reservoir" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Lorenzana from La Robla&lt;/a&gt;, average around 58 km (36 miles) and take about 3 hours. More difficult routes, like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1042304517/alto-bernesga-and-los-argueellos-biospheres-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Alto Bernesga and Los Argüellos Biospheres Loop&lt;/a&gt;, can extend over 100 km (60 miles) and require more than 5 hours to complete.

Q: Are there any routes that follow river valleys?
A: Absolutely. The Bernesga River flows through the municipality, and many roads follow its course, offering peaceful spots and fresh mountain air. Routes like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1042300095/villamanin-from-la-robla-circular-by-torio-river-and-la-profunda-mine" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Villamanín from La Robla - circular by Torío river and La Profunda Mine&lt;/a&gt; specifically highlight the Torío River, providing scenic rides through river valleys.
tips: Barrios de Luna Reservoir that was built to take advantage of the water of the Luna River to produce electricity. The Reservoir made famous by the battle of the residence of the town of Miñera de Luna to stop the construction and prevent their houses from disappearing under water like many other towns in Spain. In dry years, you can watch the ghost town rise from below.
Ruins of the medieval military strategic Castillo de Luna overlooking the Luna dam. Spectacular structure like something from a fantasy story.
Los Barrios de Luna is a small town in the shadow of the Luna Dam that retains the waters of the Luna River in this incredibly beautiful valley.

The landscape of La Robla has been marked for decades by its cement plant and its thermal power station. The first continues to operate at full capacity after a reform in 2004 that made it one of the most modern facilities of this type in Europe. The second was closed in 2020 as part of the closure of all coal-fired power plants in Spain, its two cooling towers being demolished in 2022 and radically changing the environment again, just as they did when they were built.

faqs.question: What is the difficulty level of the Embalse Barrios de Luna cycling route?
faqs.answer: This route is classified as difficult for racebike cycling. It covers 75.2 kilometers with a significant elevation gain of 1,219 meters, demanding very good fitness due to sustained climbs and descents.

faqs.question: How long does it typically take to complete this cycling route?
faqs.answer: The estimated duration for completing this 75.2-kilometer racebike route is approximately 3 hours and 48 minutes, depending on your pace and stops.

faqs.question: What kind of terrain can I expect on the Embalse Barrios de Luna circular route?
faqs.answer: The route features mostly well-paved and easy-to-ride surfaces, ideal for racebikes. However, be prepared for substantial elevation changes, including sustained climbs and descents, as you navigate the mountainous landscape.

faqs.question: What are the main points of interest along the route?
faqs.answer: You'll cycle past the scenic Barrios de Luna Reservoir, the historic Castillo de Luna, and through the charming settlements of Los Barrios de Luna and La Robla Town Center. A notable feature is the iconic suspension bridge over the reservoir.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to cycle this route?
faqs.answer: The route offers picturesque views year-round. The surrounding mountains are particularly striking when snow-covered, but for optimal cycling conditions, consider late spring through early autumn to avoid potential snow and ice on higher elevations.

faqs.question: Are there any permits or entrance fees required for this route?
faqs.answer: No, there are no specific permits or entrance fees required to cycle this route. It passes through the Reserva de la Biosfera del Alto Bernesga, which is a protected natural area, but access for cycling is generally free.

faqs.question: Where can I park for the Embalse Barrios de Luna cycling route?
faqs.answer: The route starts from Pola de Gordón. You can typically find parking within the town center or designated areas suitable for visitors. Specific parking details may vary, so check local signage upon arrival.

faqs.question: Is this cycling route dog-friendly?
faqs.answer: While cycling with dogs can be challenging on a racebike route of this difficulty and length, if you choose to bring a dog, ensure they are well-conditioned for such distances and always kept on a leash, especially in populated areas and within the Biosphere Reserve.

faqs.question: Does this route intersect with other official trails?
faqs.answer: Yes, sections of this route overlap with several official trails, including the Camino Olvidado (La Robla-Cirujales), Camino del Salvador, and Vía de la Plata, among others. This offers opportunities to explore different paths if you wish to extend or modify your ride.

faqs.question: Are there amenities like cafes or accommodation along the route in La Magdalena or La Robla?
faqs.answer: Yes, both La Magdalena and La Robla Town Center are settlements along the route where you can expect to find amenities such as cafes, pubs, and accommodation options suitable for cyclists. It's advisable to check opening hours, especially outside peak season.

faqs.question: What kind of scenery will I encounter while cycling?
faqs.answer: You'll be treated to stunning mountain scenery, picturesque views of the Barrios de Luna Reservoir, and wide, varied forests within the Alto Bernesga Biosphere Reserve. The route offers a blend of tranquil waters, dramatic mountain backdrops, and natural beauty.
